Work: Xue, Kallitsis, Houmansadr, Ensafi. "Fingerprinting Obfuscated Proxy Traffic with Encapsulated TLS Handshakes."
What it does: Protocol-agnostic detection of any obfuscated proxy by fingerprinting nested TLS handshakes inside encrypted streams; deployed at million-user ISP.
How much it weakens: Forces you to argue (correctly) that WireGuard's outer encryption defeats this signal, but the "protocol-agnostic detection of obfuscated tunnels" framing is now claimed.

Work: Xue, Stanley, Kumar, Ensafi. "The Discriminative Power of Cross-layer RTTs in Fingerprinting Proxy Traffic."
What it does: Protocol-agnostic detection via misalignment of transport- and application-layer RTTs in proxied traffic.
How much it weakens: Same — you must explain why this doesn't already silently solve your problem for any TCP-bearing flow inside WireGuard.

Work: Wu, Sippe, Sivakumar, Burg, Anderson, Wang, Bock, Houmansadr, Levin, Wustrow. "How the Great Firewall of China Detects and Blocks Fully Encrypted Traffic."
What it does: Reverse-engineers GFW's entropy / popcount / printable-prefix rules used to detect Shadowsocks/VMess/Obfs4.
How much it weakens: Methodologically adjacent for udp2raw's UDP/encrypted mode; you should adopt and cite their popcount-per-byte test. Does not evaluate udp2raw or WireGuard.

Work: Parker. "Efficacy of Full-Packet Encryption in Mitigating Protocol Detection for Evasive VPNs."
Citation: arXiv:2412.17352, Dec 2024 (preprint, single author, conference TBD).
What it does: ML-based detection of the ACC fully-encrypted protocol.
How much it weakens: Single-author non-peer-reviewed preprint; you should cite it as concurrent and explain WireGuard's structural differences from ACC.

Work: Mazel, Saudrais, Hervieu (ANSSI). "ML-based tunnel detection and tunneled application classification."
What it does: ML on flow metadata for IPsec/OpenVPN/SSH/WireGuard tunnel identification.
How much it weakens: Covers vanilla WireGuard but not obfuscated WireGuard or udp2raw. Cite for completeness.

Work: Xue, Ablove, Ramesh, Danciu, Ensafi. "Bridging Barriers: A Survey of Challenges and Priorities in the Censorship Circumvention Landscape."
What it does: Stakeholder-survey SoK from 12 leading CT providers; identifies open problems in CT ecosystem.
How much it weakens: Sets context; does not preempt technical detection work. Expect to cite it as motivation.

Work: Wendzel, Volpert, Zillien, Lenz, Rünz, Caviglione. "A Survey of Internet Censorship and its Measurement."
How much it weakens: Confirms gap; does not occupy it.

Work: Razooqi, Pekar. "Binary VPN Traffic Detection Using Wavelet Features and Machine Learning."
Mixon-Baca, Knockel, Xue, Ayyagari, Kapur, Ensafi, Crandall. "Attacking Connection Tracking Frameworks as used by Virtual Private Networks."
What it does: Port-shadow MITM attack on OpenVPN/WireGuard/OpenConnect on Linux/FreeBSD.
How much it weakens: Different problem (deanonymization, not fingerprinting). Cite to show you know the WireGuard-attack literature.

Work: Lafourcade et al. "A Tale of Two Worlds, a Formal Story of WireGuard."
How much it weakens: Cryptographic correctness, not detection. Cite once.

Severity: **NONE for your pitch but relevant context**
Work: Xue, Huremagic, Wang, Sundara Raman, Ensafi. "Fingerprinting Deep Packet Inspection Devices by Their Ambiguities."
How much it weakens: Inverse problem — useful as a methodological reference.
